page: Amazon Web Services m8a.2xlarge is a general-purpose instance featuring an AMD EPYC 9R45 processor with 8 dedicated physical cores and 8 vCPUs. Operating at a clock speed of 4.5 GHz, the x86_64 architecture CPU includes 32 MB of L3 cache and supports advanced instruction sets such as AVX-512 and cryptography acceleration via AES and SHA_NI. The instance is equipped with 32.0 GB of DDR5 memory running at 6 GHz, providing a balanced 4.0 GB of RAM per core. It operates on the Nitro hypervisor, lacks local storage, and features a baseline network bandwidth of 4 Gbps.

Benchmark data reveals top-tier single-core performance, with stress-ng single-core CPU, Geekbench single-core, and single-threaded gzip compression scoring in the top 10% of tested servers. Text processing and small-model LLM text generation also achieve top-tier throughput. However, multi-core scaling and memory bandwidth benchmarks, including multi-threaded bzip3 decompression and Passmark CPU scores, fall into the average tier.

From a resource density perspective, the m8a.2xlarge offers a balanced hardware profile for general-purpose workloads, though the lack of local NVMe storage and the modest 4 Gbps baseline network bandwidth represent notable tradeoffs for storage-heavy or network-intensive applications. This instance is best suited for workloads that benefit from strong single-core execution and modern instruction sets, such as static web serving, software compilation, and light LLM inference tasks.
description: Amazon Web Services m8a.2xlarge is a general-purpose instance powered by an 8-core AMD EPYC 9R45 processor running at 4.5 GHz on the Nitro hypervisor. It features 32.0 GB of high-speed DDR5 memory, offering a balanced 4.0 GB per core, but lacks local storage and has a baseline network bandwidth of 4 Gbps. Benchmarks show top-tier single-core CPU performance, single-threaded compression, and small-model LLM text generation, while multi-core operations and memory bandwidth remain in the average tier. Lacking local storage, this instance is designed for workloads requiring strong single-thread execution and modern instruction sets, such as static web serving, text processing, and light inference tasks.
